title: Training LLMs for Multi-Step Tool Orchestration with Constrained Data Synthesis and Graduated Rewards
author: Jiayang, Cheng, Liu, Xin, Zhang, Zhihan, Wen, Haoyang, Zhang, Zixuan, Yin, Qingyu, Li, Shiyang, Nigam, Priyanka, Yin, Bing, Zhang, Chao, Song, Yangqiu
summary: This paper addresses the challenge of training large language models to orchestrate multi-step tool use sequences, which frequently fail due to parameter errors in complex API workflows. The authors propose a reinforcement learning framework combining two innovations: a deterministic environment synthesizing valid multi-step execution traces using cached real API responses with controllable complexity, and a graduated reward function decomposing correctness into atomic validity (call-level correctness) and orchestration consistency (proper sequencing respecting dependencies). Evaluated on ComplexFuncBench and BFCL v4, the approach substantially improves turn accuracy and demonstrates transfer of learned orchestration skills across different API ecosystems, addressing a critical limitation in current LLM tool-use capabilities.
content: Computer Science > Machine Learning arXiv:2603.24709 (cs) [Submitted on 25 Mar 2026 ( v1 ), last revised 6 Apr 2026 (this version, v2)] Title: Training LLMs for Multi-Step Tool Orchestration with Constrained Data Synthesis and Graduated Rewards Authors: Cheng Jiayang , Xin Liu , Zhihan Zhang , Haoyang Wen , Zixuan Zhang , Qingyu Yin , Shiyang Li , Priyanka Nigam , Bing Yin , Chao Zhang , Yangqiu Song View a PDF of the paper titled Training LLMs for Multi-Step Tool Orchestration with Constrained Data Synthesis and Graduated Rewards, by Cheng Jiayang and 10 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: Multi-step tool orchestration remains challenging for LLMs, as state-of-the-art models frequently fail on full sequence execution due to parameter errors. Training for these workflows faces two obstacles: the lack of environments supporting complex real-world API dependencies, and sparse binary rewards that provide no signal for partial correctness. We propose a reinforcement learning framework addressing both challenges. First, we construct a deterministic environment backed by a large-scale cache of real API responses, enabling constrained synthesis of valid multi-step traces with controllable complexity. Second, we introduce a graduated reward that decomposes correctness into atomic validity (call-level correctness at increasing granularity) and orchestration consistency (correct sequencing with dependency respect). On ComplexFuncBench, our approach substantially improves turn accuracy, with ablations confirming both reward components are essential. Cross-benchmark evaluation on BFCL v4 shows that the learned orchestration skills transfer to entirely different API ecosystems (e.g., agentic web search and memory management), yielding consistent gains while maintaining stable single-step performance. Code is available at this https URL Comments: Under Review Subjects: Machine Learning (cs.LG) ; Computation and Language (cs.CL) Cite as: arXiv:2603.24709 [cs.LG] (or arXiv:2603.24709v2 [cs.LG] for this version) https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2603.24709 Focus to learn more arXiv-issued DOI via DataCite Submission history From: Xin Liu [ view email ] [v1] Wed, 25 Mar 2026 18:31:39 UTC (524 KB) [v2] Mon, 6 Apr 2026 20:50:24 UTC (526 KB) Full-text links: Access Paper: View a PDF of the paper titled Training LLMs for Multi-Step Tool Orchestration with Constrained Data Synthesis and Graduated Rewards, by Cheng Jiayang and 10 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) TeX Source view license Current browse context: cs.LG < prev | next > new | recent | 2026-03 Change to browse by: cs cs.CL References & Citations NASA ADS Google Scholar Semantic Scholar export BibTeX citation Loading... takeaways: Graduated rewards decomposing correctness into atomic validity and orchestration consistency substantially outperform sparse binary rewards for tool orchestration training.
Constrained data synthesis using cached real API responses enables generation of valid multi-step traces with controlled complexity without requiring full environment simulation.
Learned multi-step orchestration skills transfer effectively across different API ecosystems, suggesting generalizable improvements beyond domain-specific training data.
Both reward components and environment realism are essential; ablations confirm neither component can be omitted without significant performance degradation.

title: Beyond Human-Readable: Rethinking Software Engineering Conventions for the Agentic Development Era
author: Ustynov, Dmytro
summary: This arXiv paper challenges the six-decade emphasis on human-readable code conventions, arguing that agentic AI development fundamentally changes who the 'primary consumer' of code is. Ustynov proposes semantic density optimization—removing zero-information tokens while preserving high-value ones—as a key design principle. A controlled experiment on log formats reveals a counterintuitive finding: aggressive compression reduced input tokens by 17% but increased session costs by 67%, because the model spent more reasoning effort decoding compressed formats. The paper proposes rehabilitating classical anti-patterns, introducing 'program skeletons' for agent navigation, and decoupling semantic intent from human-readable presentation, suggesting that software engineering conventions must fundamentally evolve for agentic workflows.
content: Computer Science > Software Engineering arXiv:2604.07502 (cs) [Submitted on 8 Apr 2026] Title: Beyond Human-Readable: Rethinking Software Engineering Conventions for the Agentic Development Era Authors: Dmytro Ustynov View a PDF of the paper titled Beyond Human-Readable: Rethinking Software Engineering Conventions for the Agentic Development Era, by Dmytro Ustynov View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: For six decades, software engineering principles have been optimized for a single consumer: the human developer. The rise of agentic AI development, where LLM-based agents autonomously read, write, navigate, and debug codebases, introduces a new primary consumer with fundamentally different constraints. This paper presents a systematic analysis of human-centric conventions under agentic pressure and proposes a key design principle: semantic density optimization, eliminating tokens that carry zero information while preserving tokens that carry high semantic value. We validate this principle through a controlled experiment on log format token economy across four conditions (human-readable, structured, compressed, and tool-assisted compressed), demonstrating a counterintuitive finding: aggressive compression increased total session cost by 67% despite reducing input tokens by 17%, because it shifted interpretive burden to the model's reasoning phase. We extend this principle to propose the rehabilitation of classical anti-patterns, introduce the program skeleton concept for agentic code navigation, and argue for a fundamental decoupling of semantic intent from human-readable representation. Subjects: Software Engineering (cs.SE) ; Artificial Intelligence (cs.AI) Cite as: arXiv:2604.07502 [cs.SE] (or arXiv:2604.07502v1 [cs.SE] for this version) https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2604.07502 Focus to learn more arXiv-issued DOI via DataCite Submission history From: Dmytro Ustynov [ view email ] [v1] Wed, 8 Apr 2026 18:38:49 UTC (504 KB) Full-text links: Access Paper: View a PDF of the paper titled Beyond Human-Readable: Rethinking Software Engineering Conventions for the Agentic Development Era, by Dmytro Ustynov View PDF HTML (experimental) TeX Source view license Current browse context: cs.SE < prev | next > new | recent | 2026-04 Change to browse by: cs cs.AI References & Citations NASA ADS Google Scholar Semantic Scholar export BibTeX citation Loading... takeaways: Semantic density optimization: remove zero-information tokens while preserving high-value semantic content for LLM agents
Aggressive compression paradox: token reduction doesn't always lower costs; interpretive burden shifted to model reasoning increased total expense by 67%
Classical anti-patterns may be rehabilitated when optimized for agent consumption rather than human readability
Program skeleton concept enables improved agentic code navigation and understanding of codebase structure
Software engineering conventions must decouple semantic intent from human-readable representation to optimize for agentic AI development

title: Evaluating Multimodal Interactive Agents
author: Josh Abramson, Arun Ahuja, Federico Carnevale, Petko Georgiev, Alex Goldin, Jessica Landon, Timothy Lillicrap, Alistair Muldal, Adam Santoro, Tamara von Glehn, Gregory Wayne, Nathaniel Wong, Chen Yan, Blake Richards*, Alden Hung* (*Â External authors
summary: This article addresses the critical challenge of evaluating multimodal interactive agents—systems that perceive and act in complex environments using multiple sensory modalities. The authors propose comprehensive evaluation methodologies for assessing agent capabilities across planning, reasoning, and interactive behavior. The work is significant for technical researchers because standardized evaluation frameworks are essential for advancing embodied AI systems, enabling fair comparison of agent architectures, and identifying performance bottlenecks. The research provides structured approaches to benchmark agents on tasks requiring imagination, planning, and adaptive decision-making in interactive settings.
content: Agents that imagine and plan July 2017 Research Learn more
takeaways: Multimodal agents require specialized evaluation metrics beyond standard supervised learning benchmarks to assess planning and reasoning abilities.
Standardized evaluation frameworks enable fair comparison across different agent architectures and multimodal input combinations.
Interactive agent evaluation must account for environment complexity, task diversity, and real-time performance constraints.
Planning and imagination capabilities in agents should be evaluated separately from execution and control performance.
Benchmark design should include tasks requiring long-horizon reasoning and adaptive behavior in dynamic environments.

title: Code Is Cheap
summary: This piece argues that in modern software development, writing code itself has become a commodity skill—the real value lies in architectural decisions, system design, and understanding problem domains. As AI tools make code generation increasingly trivial, developers and teams must shift focus from pure coding ability to higher-order thinking: identifying the right abstractions, designing scalable systems, and making trade-offs that matter. Technical readers should care because this reframes what it means to be valuable in an AI-augmented future where coding skill alone is insufficient for career growth.
takeaways: Code generation is commoditized; architectural and design decisions now drive differentiation.
Understanding problem domains and business context matters more than syntax expertise.
AI tools enable rapid prototyping, shifting focus to validation and system correctness trade-offs.
Career value increasingly depends on judgment in design rather than lines of code written.

title: How Coding Agents Work
summary: This article explores the architectural design and operational mechanisms of coding agents—AI systems capable of autonomously writing, debugging, and testing code. The author explains how these agents integrate language models with tools for code execution, version control, and error feedback loops, enabling them to tackle complex programming tasks with minimal human intervention. Understanding coding agent architecture is critical for developers building AI-assisted development systems and for researchers advancing autonomous software engineering capabilities.
takeaways: Coding agents combine LLMs with executable sandboxes and feedback loops to iteratively refine code solutions.
Tool integration (compilers, linters, test runners) allows agents to validate code and learn from execution failures.
Agent design requires careful prompt engineering and memory management to handle multi-step reasoning over large codebases.
Error-driven learning enables agents to recover from mistakes and improve subsequent attempts.

title: Effective Harnesses For Long Running Agents
summary: This article discusses the architectural patterns and design principles needed to build harnesses that manage long-running autonomous agents effectively. The piece addresses key challenges in agent lifecycle management, including graceful shutdown mechanisms, state persistence, error recovery, and monitoring during extended operations. For technical readers building production agent systems, understanding harness design is critical—poor harness architecture leads to resource leaks, data loss, and unpredictable failures in agents that may run for hours or days without human intervention.
takeaways: Implement checkpoint-based state persistence to enable agent recovery without data loss during failures
Design harnesses with graceful shutdown that allows agents to complete critical operations before termination
Monitor resource consumption (memory, CPU, connections) continuously to prevent degradation over extended runs
Separate agent logic from harness concerns (scheduling, lifecycle, error handling) for maintainability and reusability

title: Building Effective Agents
summary: This article outlines foundational principles for designing production-ready AI agents that are reliable and maintainable. It covers key architectural patterns including message passing, state management, and error handling mechanisms that enable agents to operate effectively in real-world systems. The piece emphasizes practical patterns developers should adopt when building agents, such as designing for observability, implementing robust feedback loops, and creating clear separation of concerns. This is directly relevant to engineers currently deploying or planning to deploy agentic systems who need concrete guidance on moving beyond prototype-stage implementations.
takeaways: Design agents with message passing and clear state management for reliability and debuggability.
Implement comprehensive observability and logging to track agent behavior and failure modes.
Create explicit feedback loops and human-in-the-loop mechanisms for course correction.
Separate concerns between reasoning, planning, and execution layers.
Build agents with graceful degradation and fallback strategies for production resilience.

title: Components Of A Coding Agent
summary: This article outlines the fundamental components that constitute a coding agent architecture. It examines the key building blocks required for an AI system to autonomously write, test, and deploy code. The piece clarifies the structural design patterns necessary for agents to integrate planning, code generation, and error handling into a cohesive system. Understanding these components is essential for developers building or deploying coding agents, as it establishes a mental model for how such systems function and where potential failure points may occur.
takeaways: Coding agents require integrated planning, execution, and feedback loops to function autonomously.
Error handling and code validation are critical components that prevent invalid outputs.
Integration with version control and testing frameworks is necessary for safe code deployment.
Memory mechanisms allow agents to learn from past errors and improve over iterations.
Modular architecture enables agents to specialize in different programming domains.

title: Harness Engineering - first thoughts
author: Birgitta BÃ¶ckeler
summary: Birgitta Böckeler analyzes the concept of 'harness engineering' for maintaining large AI-generated codebases, drawing from OpenAI's recent work maintaining a 1M+ line codebase with AI agents. A harness comprises computational guides, sensors, and constraints—context engineering, architectural rules, and periodic cleanup processes—that keep AI agents aligned with codebase quality standards. Rather than aiming for full automation, well-designed harnesses direct human expertise where it matters most. Böckeler explores implications: harnesses may evolve into organization-wide service templates, AI adoption could converge tech stacks around 'AI-friendly' architectures, and runtime constraints may prove essential for maintaining code quality at scale, trading generation flexibility for reliability.
content: Harness Engineering - first thoughts Birgitta BÃ¶ckeler Birgitta is a Distinguished Engineer and AI-assisted delivery expert at Thoughtworks. She has over 20 years of experience as a software developer, architect and technical leader. This article is part of âExploring Gen AIâ . A series capturing Thoughtworks technologists' explorations of using gen ai technology for software development. 17 February 2026 Since writing this memo, I have had time to analyze the topic further and write a more considered article describing Harness Engineering . This frames the elements of a harness as guides and sensors, which may be computational or inferential. Harness templates allow us to share common guides and sensors around a larger software organization. Harnesses attempt to externalise and make explicit what human developer experience brings to the table, but they can only go so far. A good harness should not necessarily aim to fully eliminate human input, but to direct it to where our input is most important. It was very interesting to read OpenAIâs recent write-up on âHarness engineeringâ which describes how a team used âno manually typed code at allâ as a forcing function to build a harness for maintaining a large application with AI agents. After 5 months, theyâve built a real product thatâs now over 1 million lines of code. The article is titled âHarness engineering: leveraging Codex in an agent-first worldâ, but only mentions âharnessâ once in the text. Maybe the term was an afterthought inspired by Mitchell Hashimoto âs recent blog post. Either way, I like âharnessâ as a word to describe the tooling and practices we can use to keep AI agents in check. The OpenAI teamâs harness components mix deterministic and LLM-based approaches across 3 categories (grouping based on my interpretation): Context engineering : Continuously enhanced knowledge base in the codebase, plus agent access to dynamic context like observability data and browser navigation Architectural constraints : Monitored not only by the LLM-based agents, but also deterministic custom linters and structural tests âGarbage collectionâ : Agents that run periodically to find inconsistencies in documentation or violations of architectural constraints, fighting entropy and decay They also highlight how iterative this is: âWhen the agent struggles, we treat it as a signal: identify what is missing â tools, guardrails, documentation â and feed it back into the repository, always by having Codex itself write the fix.â All of the described measures focus on increasing long-term internal quality and maintainability. What I am missing in the write-up is verification of functionality and behaviour. Leaving that gap aside, and assuming we can trust OpenAIâs representation of the success of this (with respect to the author and the team, OpenAI do have a vested interest in us believing in AI-maintainable code) â here are my thoughts on what is in the article. Harnesses - the future service templates? Most organizations have just two or three main tech stacks â not every application is its own snowflake. The article made me imagine a future where teams pick from a set of harnesses for common application topologies to get started. This evokes todayâs service templates, which help teams instantiate new services on a âgolden pathâ. Will harnesses â with custom linters, structural tests, basic context and knowledge documentation, and additional context providers â become the new service templates? Will teams use them as a starting point, then shape them over time for their applicationâs specifics? With service templates, teams contribute back as they gain experience, then other teams often struggle to incorporate updates. Would we see a similar forking and synchronization challenge with harnesses? The article also made me revisit some of my older hypotheses: The runtime has to be constrained for more AI autonomy? A lot of early and current AI coding hype assumes LLMs will give us unlimited flexibility of the target runtime. Generate in any language, any pattern, without constraints â the LLM will figure it out. But for maintainable, AI-generated code at scale that we can trust, something has to give. The harness described suggests that increasing trust and reliability required constraining the solution space: specific architectural patterns, enforced boundaries, standardized structures. That means giving up some âgenerate anythingâ flexibility for prompts, rules, and harnesses full of technical specifics. A convergence on a limited number of tech stacks and topologies? As coding becomes less about typing code and more about steering its generation, AI might push us toward fewer tech stacks. Usability of frameworks and SDKs still matters â weâre seeing repeatedly that whatâs good for humans is good for AI. But developer tastes will matter less at that level of detail. Little inefficiencies and idiosyncracies in interfaces will be less annoying since we donât deal with them directly. We might choose stacks with good harnesses available and prioritize âAI-friendlinessâ. This might apply not just to tech stacks, but also to codebase structures and topologies. We might default to structures that are easier to maintain with AI because theyâre easier to harness. The OpenAI team discusses architectural rigidity and enforcement rules. The main focus areas I can see are keeping data structures stable and defining and enforcing module boundaries. Sounds reasonable â but without concrete examples, Iâm still struggling to imagine what âwe require Codex to parse data shapes at the boundaryâ looks like in practice in their harness. But if we can figure out widely how to harness codebase design patterns, will these topologies become the new abstraction layer, not natural language itself like so many AI enthusiasts hope? Two future worlds: Pre-AI vs post-AI application maintenance? Say we develop good harnessing techniques to turn AI autonomy up to 9 and increase our confidence in the results. Which techniques could we apply to existing applications, and which would only work for applications built from scratch with a harness in mind? For older codebases, weâd need to consider whether retrofitting a harness is worth the effort. AI can help us do that faster, but those applications are often so non-standardized and full of entropy that it might not be worthwhile. It makes me think of running a static code analysis tool on a codebase thatâs never had one, and then drowning in alerts. Whatâs your harness today? That this team worked on their harness for 5 months shows this isnât something you can jump into for quick results. But itâs worth reflecting on what your harness is today. Do you have a pre-commit hook? Whatâs in it? Do you have ideas for custom linters? What architectural constraints would you like to impose on your codebase? Have you experimented with structural testing frameworks like ArchUnit? Final thoughts Unsurprisingly, what they describe sounds like much more work than just generating and maintaining a bunch of Markdown rules files. They built extensive tooling for the deterministic part of the harness. Their context engineering involved not only curating a knowledge base, but also significant design work â the code design itself is a huge part of the context. The OpenAI team says: âOur most difficult challenges now center on designing environments, feedback loops, and control systems.â This reminded me of Chad Fowlerâs recent post on âRelocating Rigorâ . Itâs refreshing to hear concrete ideas and experiences about where that rigor might go, rather than just hoping âbetter modelsâ will magically solve maintainability issues. And finally, for once, I like a term in this space. Though itâs only 2 weeks old â I can probably hold my metaphorical breath until somebody calls their one-prompt, LLM-based code review agent a harnessâ¦ latest article (Mar 04): Humans and Agents in Software Engineering Loops previous article: Context Engineering for Coding Agents next article: Humans and Agents in Software Engineering Loops
takeaways: Harnesses combine context engineering, architectural constraints, and periodic 'garbage collection' to keep AI agents productive and codebase quality high
Runtime and architectural constraints increase trustworthiness of AI-generated code; unlimited flexibility trades off against maintainability at scale
Future may see convergence on fewer tech stacks chosen for AI-friendliness and harness availability rather than developer preference
Service templates could evolve into harnesses—shared guides, linters, tests, and context—adopted across organizations but facing similar forking/sync challenges

title: Ty Building Effective AI Agents
summary: This article examines the architectural principles and design patterns necessary for building reliable, production-grade AI agents. It discusses how to structure agents that can reliably perform complex tasks, integrate with external systems, and maintain consistency across interactions. The piece emphasizes the importance of thoughtful design in agent architecture, covering aspects like tool integration, state management, and error handling that distinguish experimental agents from those suitable for real-world deployment.
takeaways: Design agents with clear tool interfaces and proper error handling for production reliability
Implement robust state management to track context and prevent inconsistent agent behavior
Separate concerns between reasoning, planning, and execution layers in agent architecture
Validate agent outputs and maintain audit trails for safety and debugging
Plan for graceful degradation when external tools or APIs become unavailable
